text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic low anterior resection by dissecting the mesentery from caudal to cranial direction, creating a mesenteric window to facilitate further surgical access.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein, ligates it with two hemoclips, and then cuts it. The inferior mesenteric artery is similarly ligated and cut.

The ureter is visible below the surgical field. A vascular plane is identified on the posterior aspect of the rectum, and dissection continues on the lateral side. The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt.
The surgeon achieves complete mobilization of the left colon, with the spine visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon encounters adhesions in the abdominal cavity, likely due to previous surgeries. A suspicious area for endometriosis is visually identified, prompting the performance of a colonoscopy for confirmation.
The surgeon confirms the correct location for the procedure and determines clean surgical margins. The rectum is prepared for anastomosis, ensuring precise alignment for the upcoming connection.
The surgeon transects the distal rectum using GA steppers, ensuring preservation of the left ureter, which shows signs of fibrosis. Hemostasis is checked to confirm no bleeding.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler, visually aligning and joining the rectal and colonic ends.
